conflicts.reason: Vitamin C (low pH ~3) destabilizes retinol (needs pH 5.5-6.5). Use Vitamin C in AM and Retinol in PM.
conflicts.suggestion: Use Vitamin C in your morning routine and Retinol at night.

conflicts.reason: Benzoyl peroxide oxidizes and deactivates retinol, making both less effective.
conflicts.suggestion: Alternate nights: Retinol one night, Benzoyl Peroxide the next.

conflicts.reason: Both increase cell turnover aggressively. Together they can cause severe irritation, peeling, and barrier damage.
conflicts.suggestion: Use AHA on different nights than retinol, or choose one.

conflicts.reason: Both can be drying and irritating together, especially for sensitive skin. Some people tolerate this combo.
conflicts.suggestion: If combining, use salicylic acid as a cleanser (wash-off) and retinol as a leave-on.

conflicts.reason: Both exfoliate — layering them can over-exfoliate and damage the skin barrier.
conflicts.suggestion: Alternate nights or use lactic acid in AM and retinol in PM.

conflicts.reason: Benzoyl peroxide is a strong oxidizer that neutralizes Vitamin C (an antioxidant), canceling its benefits.
conflicts.suggestion: Use Vitamin C in AM and Benzoyl Peroxide in PM.

conflicts.reason: Both work at low pH but layering two acids can irritate. Vitamin C can become unstable.
conflicts.suggestion: Use on alternate mornings, or apply glycolic acid as a toner and wait 15 min before Vitamin C.

conflicts.reason: Both are potent acne treatments. Together they can excessively dry and irritate skin.
conflicts.suggestion: Use BP in the morning and SA in the evening, or alternate days.

conflicts.reason: Mixing AHA + BHA can over-exfoliate. However, some products are formulated with both safely.
conflicts.suggestion: If using separately, alternate AM/PM or use on different days.

conflicts.reason: Both can cause irritation and sensitivity. The combination may overwhelm sensitive skin.
conflicts.suggestion: Introduce one at a time. If using both, apply kojic acid in AM and retinol in PM.

conflicts.reason: Old myth! Modern research shows these work fine together. Early studies used impure niacinamide at high heat.
conflicts.suggestion: Safe to use together. They actually complement each other for brightening!

conflicts.reason: BP is an oxidizer; Vitamin E is an antioxidant. They can neutralize each other.
conflicts.suggestion: Apply at different times of day.

conflicts.reason: Both promote cell turnover. Combining may lead to excessive exfoliation.
conflicts.suggestion: Use mandelic acid on non-retinol nights.

synergies.benefit: Vitamin E regenerates Vitamin C and doubles its photoprotective efficacy. The gold standard antioxidant combo.
Ferulic acid stabilizes Vitamin C and boosts its antioxidant power by 8x. The famous CE Ferulic formula.
Niacinamide strengthens the barrier while HA draws in moisture. Perfect duo for plump, healthy skin.
Both control oil and reduce inflammation. Together they're one of the best combos for oily acne-prone skin.
HA attracts water while ceramides lock it in. The ultimate hydration sandwich.
HA provides hydration that counteracts retinol's drying effects, making it more tolerable.
Peptides boost collagen alongside retinol. Using both accelerates anti-aging results.
Both soothe inflammation. Centella heals while niacinamide strengthens — great for post-treatment recovery.
Both fight hyperpigmentation through different mechanisms. Together, faster fade of dark spots.
SA unclogs pores while niacinamide reduces oil and inflammation. Great for acne routine when layered.
Both humectants working at different skin layers — HA at surface, glycerin penetrates deeper.
Both inhibit melanin through different pathways. Combined, they give faster brightening results.
Bakuchiol provides retinol-like anti-aging while HA maintains hydration. Perfect for sensitive skin anti-aging.
Both are powerful antioxidants that complement each other's free radical scavenging abilities.
Panthenol helps repair the skin barrier while ceramides restore its structure. Great for damaged skin.
Both target hyperpigmentation through different mechanisms for accelerated spot fading.
Snail mucin naturally contains HA plus growth factors. Layering HA underneath boosts total hydration.

cautions.note: Both work at low pH. Layer carefully — apply vitamin C first, wait 15 min, then SA.
Can be used together but introduce gradually. Some derms recommend alternating AM/PM.
Niacinamide can flush at very low pH. Apply glycolic acid first, wait 10 min, then niacinamide.
Generally fine together — Vitamin E can actually stabilize retinol. But patch test first.
Both are AHAs — using two AHAs together is unnecessary and can over-exfoliate.
